On "the people feared the LORD, and believed the LORD," interpreters expound the faith. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the response — "the people feared the LORD, and believed the LORD": the people, seeing the great deliverance, feared the LORD (had a reverent awe of his power and majesty) and believed the LORD (trusted him as the true and only God, faithful to his word); the fruit of the deliverance (the fear and faith of the redeemed); the witnesses note both true effects — the holy fear (reverence, awe of God) and the faith (trust, belief in God and his word); yet some note the faith was, in many, but a temporary impression (soon to fail in the murmurings, Exodus 15:24; 16:2; Psalm 106:12-13, "then believed they his words... they soon forgat his works"); the right response in itself (fear and faith), the abiding lesson (the deliverance works the fear and faith of God's people).
